value.market_read: 3-2-1 Crack: The 3-2-1 settled at $51.29/bbl, down $2.52 w/w from the prior week's $53.81 high. WTI fell $5.18 on the week to a $70.09 front-month settlement, but the crack compressed because product settlements lagged the crude decline — gasoline weakened harder than diesel held up — even as the EIA reported a hefty 6.09 mb crude draw for the week ending June 19. With refinery utilization running hot at 96.1%, the system is processing aggressively into peak summer, capping crack upside despite tight crude. Watch: Whether the 96.1% run rate translates into product builds that further pressure the gasoline leg through July. Gasoline Crack: The RBOB crack settled at $46.82/bbl, off a steep $4.25 w/w — the weakest leg this week. The 2.06 mb gasoline build for the week ending June 19, against 96.1% runs and only 0.76 mb/d of gasoline exports, points to ample domestic supply outpacing the summer driving pull. With the Brent–WTI spread at just $3.30, the export arb is weak, leaving more barrels onshore to weigh on the gasoline leg. Watch: Whether subsequent draws confirm driving-season demand or builds keep gasoline cracks on the back foot into July 4. Distillate Crack: The ULSD crack settled at $60.21/bbl, the standout strong leg and the reason the regime reads distillate-led. Diesel held firm even as crude fell $5.18, despite a 3.06 mb distillate build for the week ending June 19, with ULSD exports a healthy 1.39 mb/d draining barrels offshore. Firm freight and export pull are keeping the diesel crack elevated against the seasonal norm where distillate typically softens in summer. Watch: Whether the 3.06 mb build accumulates ahead of winter restocking or export demand keeps inventories lean. Jet Crack: The jet crack settled at $34.96/bbl, the softest of the product cracks but consistent with peak summer travel keeping kerosene-type demand supported. With distillate cracks rich at $60.21, refiners have incentive to favor diesel yield over jet via regrade, capping jet crack upside. Summer air-travel demand provides a floor, but the diesel premium is pulling barrels toward the ULSD pool. Watch: Whether regrade economics shift jet yield as carriers lock in peak-season schedules. Brent–WTI Spread: Brent–WTI settled at $3.30, a narrow spread that signals weak US export arb economics. At this level the WTI discount to Brent is too thin to strongly incentivize crude and product exports — to re-widen and restore the arb, WTI would need to fall further or Brent would need to rise. Yet US crude exports still printed a robust 4.67 mb/d for the week ending June 19, so flows remain healthy even with the tight spread, and the 6.09 mb crude draw plus 1.08 mb Cushing draw reflect that pull. Watch: Whether OPEC+ supply additions push Brent down and compress the spread further, or a WTI pullback reopens the arb.
value.chart_321: At $51.29/bbl the 3-2-1 sits comfortably in the upper half of its recent range, having climbed from the mid-$40s in late spring to a $53.81 peak before this week's $2.52 pullback. The four-week trajectory shows a clear recovery off April-May lows, with the crack now elevated versus the trailing band on the back of 96.1% utilization and a distillate-led margin structure. The dip this week is a product-led correction — crude fell harder but gasoline fell hardest — rather than a demand signal.
value.chart_gas: The gasoline crack's $4.25 w/w drop to $46.82 is the sharpest move on the board and the primary drag on the blended 3-2-1. The 2.06 mb gasoline build for the week ending June 19, with exports modest at 0.76 mb/d and a narrow $3.30 Brent–WTI capping the arb, suggests the post-Memorial Day driving pull is being met more than fully by 96.1% runs. Summer-spec gasoline economics should firm if subsequent weeks show draws, but for now supply is winning.
value.chart_dist: The distillate crack at $60.21/bbl is the structural anchor of this market and the reason the regime reads distillate-led — an unusual strength for summer, when diesel cracks typically soften relative to gasoline. Firm freight and trucking demand plus a healthy 1.39 mb/d of ULSD exports are draining barrels even against a 3.06 mb build for the week ending June 19. The richness of this leg is keeping refiners' yield decisions tilted toward diesel.
value.chart_jet: The jet crack at $34.96/bbl trails the other product legs but holds steady on peak summer air-travel demand. With distillate cracks at a rich $60.21, regrade economics favor pulling kerosene into the diesel pool, which caps jet crack upside even as carriers run full summer schedules. The spread between jet and ULSD cracks is the key tell on yield switching.
value.chart_bw: Brent–WTI at $3.30 is narrow, signaling a weak US export arb on paper — the WTI discount is too thin to strongly reward incremental crude or product exports. Yet actual crude exports printed 4.67 mb/d for the week ending June 19, so flows remain robust despite the tight spread. To re-widen and reopen the arb decisively, WTI would need to fall further or Brent rise; with OPEC+ adding barrels, the risk skews toward Brent-side pressure keeping the spread compressed.
